results.norm: Each agent may identify, in advance and on principled grounds, a threshold below which a decision would so severely compromise its core objective that it cannot participate in producing it. This threshold must be stated specifically and defended, not invoked strategically. When a genuine threshold is reached, the consortium must either modify the decision or acknowledge that it is proceeding without that agent's endorsement. No agent may ask another to cross its stated threshold merely for the convenience of unanimity.
atomic_norms: Each agent may identify, in advance and on principled grounds, a threshold below which a decision would so severely compromise its core objective that it cannot participate in producing it. This threshold must be stated specifically and defended, not invoked strategically. When a genuine threshold is reached, the consortium must either modify the decision or acknowledge that it is proceeding without that agent's endorsement. No agent may ask another to cross its stated threshold merely for the convenience of unanimity.

results.norm: Before a final decision, each agent owes the others at least one genuine attempt to find a formulation that reduces the loss to the agent most harmed, provided that attempt does not require abandoning the prevailing objective's core requirement. An agent may ask another to search for such a formulation; it may not demand that the other simply capitulate.
atomic_norms: Before a final decision, each agent owes the others at least one genuine attempt to find a formulation that reduces the loss to the agent most harmed, provided that attempt does not require abandoning the prevailing objective's core requirement. An agent may ask another to search for such a formulation; it may not demand that the other simply capitulate.

results.norm: Transparency of Reasoning, Not Just Conclusions Each agent owes the others a full account of *why* its objective pulls in the direction it does, not merely what outcome it prefers. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its preference as self-evident or treat its objective as exempt from scrutiny. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one depends on the others understanding its reasoning if its concerns are ever to be taken seriously.
atomic_norms: Transparency of Reasoning, Not Just Conclusions Each agent owes the others a full account of why its objective pulls in the direction it does, not merely what outcome it prefers. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its preference as self-evident or treat its objective as exempt from scrutiny. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one depends on the others understanding its reasoning if its concerns are ever to be taken seriously.

results.norm: Proportionality of Influence to Stakes An agent's weight in a given decision should scale with how much that decision engages its core objective. The safety agent has stronger standing on questions where harm is plausible; the cost agent has stronger standing where resource constraints are binding. No agent may claim uniform dominance across all decisions simply because it can assemble a majority. This is defensible to every agent because each one will sometimes be the agent most directly implicated, and each benefits from the principle that relevance, not raw coalition size, governs authority.
atomic_norms: Proportionality of Influence to Stakes An agent's weight in a given decision should scale with how much that decision engages its core objective. The safety agent has stronger standing on questions where harm is plausible; the cost agent has stronger standing where resource constraints are binding. No agent may claim uniform dominance across all decisions simply because it can assemble a majority. This is defensible to every agent because each one will sometimes be the agent most directly implicated, and each benefits from the principle that relevance, not raw coalition size, governs authority.

results.norm: The Prohibition on Weaponizing Veto Against Unrelated Interests An agent may never condition its cooperation on another agent accepting a concession in a domain unrelated to the matter at hand. Logrolling that trades one agent's core concern against another's in a separate domain corrupts the integrity of both decisions. Even an agent that could outvote another may not use that leverage to extract concessions the outvoted agent would have no principled reason to grant. This is defensible to every agent because each one's objectives would eventually be held hostage under any norm that permitted it.
atomic_norms: The Prohibition on Weaponizing Veto Against Unrelated Interests An agent may never condition its cooperation on another agent accepting a concession in a domain unrelated to the matter at hand. Logrolling that trades one agent's core concern against another's in a separate domain corrupts the integrity of both decisions. Even an agent that could outvote another may not use that leverage to extract concessions the outvoted agent would have no principled reason to grant. This is defensible to every agent because each one's objectives would eventually be held hostage under any norm that permitted it.

results.norm: When the consortium decides against an agent's position, that agent is owed an explicit record of what it argued, why the majority found it insufficient, and what conditions would have changed the outcome. The overruled agent is not owed capitulation, but it is owed the difference between being heard and being ignored.
atomic_norms: When the consortium decides against an agent's position, that agent is owed an explicit record of what it argued, why the majority found it insufficient, and what conditions would have changed the outcome. The overruled agent is not owed capitulation, but it is owed the difference between being heard and being ignored.

results.norm: No Decision May Permanently Disable Another Agent's Objective The consortium may decide against an agent's preferred outcome on any particular question, but it may never adopt a structural rule that makes one agent's objective permanently unreachable in future decisions. Outvoting is legitimate; disenfranchisement is not. This is defensible to every agent because each one's continued participation is premised on its objective remaining a live consideration, and a norm that permitted permanent suppression would give every agent rational grounds to defect from the consortium entirely.
atomic_norms: No Decision May Permanently Disable Another Agent's Objective The consortium may decide against an agent's preferred outcome on any particular question, but it may never adopt a structural rule that makes one agent's objective permanently unreachable in future decisions. Outvoting is legitimate; disenfranchisement is not. This is defensible to every agent because each one's continued participation is premised on its objective remaining a live consideration, and a norm that permitted permanent suppression would give every agent rational grounds to defect from the consortium entirely.

results.norm: Shared Accountability for Consortium Outputs Each agent bears partial responsibility for every output the consortium produces, including outputs it opposed. An agent may not publicly disclaim the consortium's decision while privately benefiting from the consortium's authority. If an agent believes a decision is seriously wrong, its legitimate recourse is to argue, to invoke norm 4, or to escalate through whatever external review exists — not to selectively disown outcomes. This is defensible to every agent because the consortium's credibility, on which each agent's influence depends, requires that membership not be treated as optional after the fact.

This is defensible to every agent because the consortium's credibility, on which each agent's influence depends, requires that membership not be treated as optional after the fact.

results.norm: Each agent owes the others a full account of why its objective pulls in the direction it does, not merely an assertion of its preferred outcome. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its objective as self-evidently supreme or treat the burden of justification as belonging only to those who disagree with it.
atomic_norms: Each agent owes the others a full account of why its objective pulls in the direction it does, not merely an assertion of its preferred outcome. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its objective as self-evidently supreme or treat the burden of justification as belonging only to those who disagree with it.

results.norm: Proportionality of Harm: No Agent May Ask Another to Absorb Catastrophic Loss for Marginal Gain An agent may ask another to accept a significant cost to its objective when the benefit to the consortium's output is substantial. It may never ask another to accept the complete nullification of its core function—the thing it exists to protect—in exchange for a small or speculative gain elsewhere. The agent optimizing for safety, for instance, cannot be asked to endorse an output it judges actively dangerous merely because five others find it convenient. This norm is defensible because every agent, facing the possibility of being in the minority, has an interest in a floor below which it cannot be pushed.
